questions.question: What is the difference between delay and jitter?
options: Delay is the absolute transit time; jitter is the variation in transit time between packets
Jitter is always larger than delay
Delay only affects video, jitter only affects audio
questions.explanation: Delay is how long a packet takes to arrive. Jitter is how much that delay varies from packet to packet. The playout buffer absorbs jitter, not delay.

questions.question: What happens when a packet arrives after its scheduled playout time?
options: It is buffered for the next frame
It is too late and must be discarded (late loss)
questions.explanation: A late packet has missed its playout deadline. In real-time media it cannot be used, so it is discarded as a late loss. This is different from network packet loss.

questions.question: What is the fundamental tradeoff in choosing playout delay?
options: Latency vs late-packet loss: more delay means less loss but higher latency
questions.explanation: A larger playout buffer absorbs more jitter (fewer late packets) but adds latency. A smaller buffer reduces latency but increases the chance of late loss.

questions.question: Why does adaptive playout delay adjust during talkspurt boundaries?
options: Because the network is faster during silence
To avoid audible glitches: adjusting during silence makes the change imperceptible
Because packets are larger during talkspurts
It does not matter when adjustment happens
questions.explanation: Adjusting playout delay during a talkspurt would stretch or compress speech, causing an audible glitch. Adjusting during silence (between talkspurts) makes the change imperceptible.
